name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
أَوَمَن كَانَ مَيْتًا فَأَحْيَيْنَـٰهُ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُۥ نُورًا يَمْشِى بِهِۦ فِى ٱلنَّاسِ كَمَن مَّثَلُهُۥ فِى ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ لَيْسَ بِخَارِجٍ مِّنْهَا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ زُيِّنَ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٢٢)
```

```english
Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and provided him with a light by which he walks among the people, like one who dwells in a manifold darkness which he cannot leave? To the faithless is thus presented as decorous what they have been doing. (122)
```

```arabic
وَكَذَٰلِكَ جَعَلْنَا فِى كُلِّ قَرْيَةٍ أَكَـٰبِرَ مُجْرِمِيهَا لِيَمْكُرُوا۟ فِيهَا ۖ وَمَا يَمْكُرُونَ إِلَّا بِأَنفُسِهِمْ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ (١٢٣)
```

```english
Thus have We installed in every town its major criminals that they may plot therein. Yet they do not plot except against their own souls, but they are not aware. (123)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا جَآءَتْهُمْ ءَايَةٌ قَالُوا۟ لَن نُّؤْمِنَ حَتَّىٰ نُؤْتَىٰ مِثْلَ مَآ أُوتِىَ رُسُلُ ٱللَّهِ ۘ ٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ حَيْثُ يَجْعَلُ رِسَالَتَهُۥ ۗ سَيُصِيبُ ٱلَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا۟ صَغَارٌ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَعَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَمْكُرُونَ (١٢٤)
```

```english
When a sign comes to them, they say, ‘We will not believe until we are given the like of what was given to Allah’s apostles.’ Allah knows best where to place His apostleship! Soon the guilty will be visited by a degradation and severe punishment from Allah because of the plots they used to devise. (124)
```

```arabic
فَمَن يُرِدِ ٱللَّهُ أَن يَهْدِيَهُۥ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُۥ لِلْإِسْلَـٰمِ ۖ وَمَن يُرِدْ أَن يُضِلَّهُۥ يَجْعَلْ صَدْرَهُۥ ضَيِّقًا حَرَجًا كَأَنَّمَا يَصَّعَّدُ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَجْعَلُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلرِّجْسَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٢٥)
```

```english
Whomever Allah desires to guide, He opens his breast to Islam, and whomever He desires to lead astray, He makes his breast narrow and straitened as if he were climbing to a height. Thus does Allah lay [spiritual] defilement on those who do not have faith. (125)
```

```arabic
وَهَـٰذَا صِرَٰطُ رَبِّكَ مُسْتَقِيمًا ۗ قَدْ فَصَّلْنَا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَذَّكَّرُونَ (١٢٦)
```

```english
This is the straight path of your Lord. We have already elaborated the signs for a people who take admonition. (126)
```

```arabic
۞ لَهُمْ دَارُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمِ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ ۖ وَهُوَ وَلِيُّهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٢٧)
```

```english
For them shall be the abode of peace near their Lord and He will be their guardian because of what they used to do. (127)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَنْ مَثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُماتِ لَيْسَ بِخارِجٍ مِنْها** (Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and provided him with a light by which he walks among the people, like one who is in a manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?) [6:122]. The meaning of the verse is clear. According to what first comes to a simple and naive understanding, it is a parable (المثل) struck for both the believer and the disbeliever, through which the reality of their respective states in guidance (الهدى) and misguidance (الضلال) becomes apparent upon reflection.

For man, before being touched by divine guidance (الهدى الإلهي), is like a dead person deprived of the blessing of life, possessing neither sensation nor movement. If he believes in his Lord with a faith (إيماناً) that He approves of, he becomes like one whom Allah has brought to life after his death, and for whom He has provided a light (نوراً) that revolves with him wherever he turns. By its radiance, he perceives his good from his evil and his benefit from his harm; thus, he takes what benefits him and leaves what harms him, and in this manner, he proceeds on the path of life.

As for the disbeliever (الكافر), he is like one who has fallen into darknesses (ظلمات) from which there is no exit and no escape—the darkness of death and the subsequent darknesses of ignorance (الجهل) in the stage of distinguishing good from evil and the beneficial from the harmful. Similar to this verse in meaning, in a certain respect, is His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّما يَسْتَجِيبُ الَّذِينَ يَسْمَعُونَ وَالْمَوْتى يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللهُ** (Only those who listen will respond. As for the dead, Allah will raise them) [6:36], and His saying, the Exalted: **مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُ حَياةً طَيِّبَةً** (Whoever acts righteously, [whether] male or female, should he be faithful, We shall surely revive him with a good life) [16:97].

Thus, in this discourse, there is a metaphor (استعارة) of death for misguidance, and a metaphor of life for faith (الإيمان) or being guided (الاهتداء); and bringing to life (الإحياء) for guidance toward faith; and light (النور) for insight (التبصر) into righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة); and darkness (الظلمة) for ignorance. All of this is at the level of general instruction and understanding, for the people of this worldly context do not perceive for man, qua man, any life beyond the animal life (الحياة الحيوانية), which is the source of sensing material pleasures and voluntary movement toward them.

These people see that the believer and the disbeliever do not differ in this gift, as it is shared equally between them. Therefore, inevitably, counting the believer as alive with the life of faith, possessing a light by which he walks among the people, and counting the disbeliever as dead with the death of misguidance in darknesses from which there is no exit, is based solely on an imaginative consideration (عناية تخييلية) and a representational metaphor (استعارة تمثيلية) that represents the reality of the intended meaning.

However, reflection (التدبر) upon the various aspects of the discourse and contemplation (التأمل) of what the Noble Quran makes known, yields a meaning for the verse beyond that which common understanding attains. For Allah, the Glorified, ascribes to the divine man in His speech an eternal, everlasting life (حياة خالدة أبدية) that is not severed by worldly death. In it, he is under the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah, protected by His care, and shielded by His preservation. Neither fatigue (نصب) nor weariness (لغوب) touches him, nor does wretchedness or toil abase him. He is immersed in the love of his Lord, rejoicing in the delight of proximity (القرب), seeing nothing but good and encountering nothing but felicity (السعادة). He is in security and peace (أمن وسلام) with which there is no fear or danger, and in a happiness, delight, and pleasure that have no depletion and no end to their term.

One who is of this station sees what people do not see, hears what they do not hear, intellects (يعقل) what they do not intellect, and wills what they do not will. Even if the outward forms of his actions and the images of his movements and stillness mimic the actions, movements, and stillness of others and resemble them, he possesses a consciousness (شعور) and a will (إرادة) above the consciousness and will of others. He possesses that life which is the source of consciousness and will, which other people do not possess. Thus, the believer has a degree of life (مرتبة من الحياة) that others do not have.

Just as the commonality of humans, while sharing with other animals the consciousness of the necessities of life and voluntary movement toward them—and the animals share this with them—we nevertheless do not doubt that the human is a higher species than other animal species and possesses a life above the life they possess, due to the wondrous effects we see in man emanating from universal thoughts (الأفكار الكلية) and the intellection (تعقلات) specific to him. For this reason, we judge regarding the animal when compared to the plant, and the plant when compared to the levels of existence (مراتب الكون) preceding it, that each of them has a higher standing and a life that is more elevated than the life of what preceded it.

Therefore, let us judge regarding the human who has been given knowledge (العلم) and faith (الإيمان), who has settled in the abode of certainty (دار الإيقان), and has occupied himself with his Lord, becoming free and finding rest from all else—while he perceives what is not within the capacity of others and wills what none but he can attain—that he possesses a life above the life of others, and a light (نوراً) from which he draws support in his consciousness, and a will (إرادة) that exists only with him and within the context of his life.

Allah, the Glorified, says: **فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُ حَياةً طَيِّبَةً** (We shall surely revive him with a good life) [16:97]. Thus, they have life, but it is by its nature "good" (طيبة), beyond absolute life. And He says: **لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ لا يَفْقَهُونَ بِها وَلَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ لا يُبْصِرُونَ بِها وَلَهُمْ آذانٌ لا يَسْمَعُونَ بِها أُولئِكَ كَالْأَنْعامِ بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْغافِلُونَ** (They have hearts with which they do not understand, and they have eyes with which they do not see, and they have ears with which they do not hear. They are like cattle; indeed, they are more astray. It is they who are the heedless) [7:179]. Thus, He affirms for them the likes of hearts, eyes, and ears that are in the believers, but He negates the perfection of their effects which are found in the believers. He did not stop at that, but even affirmed for the believers a spirit (روحاً) specific to them, saying: **أُولئِكَ كَتَبَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْإِيمانَ وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِرُوحٍ مِنْهُ** (He has written faith into their hearts and strengthened them with a spirit from Him) [58:22].

It becomes clear from this that life, as well as light, has a real reality (حقيقة واقعية) in the believer, and the discourse is not proceeding upon that figurative usage (التجوز) which does not exceed the station of linguistic consideration (العناية اللفظية). Thus, the specific attribute (الصفة الخاصة) possessed by the elite of Allah among the believers is more deserving of the name "life" than the meaning of life found among the general public, just as the life of a human is so in relation to the life of an animal, and the life of an animal is so in relation to the life of a plant.

**So His saying:** **أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ** (Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life) means: astray (ضالاً) in terms of his own self, or an astray disbeliever before believing in his Lord—which is a type of death—so We brought him to life with the life of faith or guidance—the outcome being the same. **وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً** (and provided him with a light) meaning: knowledge (علماً) born from his faith, as the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said in what has been narrated by both sects: "Whoever acts upon what he knows, Allah grants him the knowledge of what he did not know—or Allah teaches him what he did not know." For when the spirit of faith (روح الإيمان) takes hold of the human soul and settles within it, it transforms views and actions into forms that suit it and do not oppose it. Likewise, all other ingrained traits (الملكات), whether virtues or vices, once they settle in the inner being of man, do not delay in transforming his views and actions into forms that mimic them.

It has been said that what is meant by "light" is faith or the Quran, but this is far-fetched given the context (السياق).

The effect of this light in the believer is that he **يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ** (walks by it among the people), meaning he gains insight (يتبصر) through it in the path of his dark social life, so that he may take from actions what benefits him in the felicity of his life and leave what harms him.

This is the state of the believer in his life and his light. Is he then **كَمَنْ مَثَلُهُ** (like one whose parable) and description is that he is **فِي الظُّلُماتِ** (in the darknesses)—the darknesses of misguidance and the loss of the light of faith—**لَيْسَ بِخارِجٍ مِنْها** (from which he cannot emerge)? This is because death does not entail the effects of life at all, so there is no hope that the disbeliever will be guided to actions that benefit him in his hereafter or bring him felicity in his end.

It has become clear from what preceded that His saying: **كَمَنْ مَثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُماتِ** etc., is in the estimation of: "he is in the darknesses, not emerging from them." Thus, in the discourse, there is a deleted subject (مبتدأ محذوف), which is the pronoun referring back to the relative pronoun (الموصول). It has also been said that the estimation is: "like the parable of one who is in the darknesses," and there is no harm in this were it not for the excessive estimation (التقدير).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَذلِكَ زُيِّنَ لِلْكافِرِينَ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** (Thus to the faithless is their conduct made to seem fair) [6:122]. The apparent context of the beginning of the verse suggests that the comparison in His saying **كَذلِكَ** (Thus) is of the type of comparing the branch to the root, with the intent of providing a universal rule (القاعدة الكلية), like His saying, the Exalted: **كَذلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللهُ الْحَقَّ وَالْباطِلَ** (Thus does Allah draw the parable of truth and falsehood) and His saying: **كَذلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللهُ الْأَمْثالَ** (Thus does Allah draw the parables) [13:17]. That is: take what We have mentioned of the parable as a root (أصلاً) and measure against it every parable you encounter. So the meaning of His saying **كَذلِكَ زُيِّنَ** (Thus is made to seem fair) etc., based on this mentioned example, is that just as the disbeliever has no exit from the darknesses, their deeds have been made to seem fair to the disbelievers with a beautification (زينة) that attracts them and imprisons them, never letting them emerge from it into the space of felicity and the expanse of light. And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people.

It has been said that the aspect of comparison in His saying **كَذلِكَ زُيِّنَ** etc., is that disbelief was made to seem fair to these people so they performed it, just as faith was made to seem fair to those others so they performed it. Thus, the state of these people in the beautification was compared to the state of those others in it. (End of quote). This is far-fetched given the context of the beginning of the verse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَكَذلِكَ جَعَلْنا فِي كُلِّ قَرْيَةٍ أَكابِرَ مُجْرِمِيها** (And thus have We installed in every town the major sinners...) [6:123] to the end of the verse. It is as if the meaning of the verse is: We have brought to life a group and provided them with a light by which they walk among the people, and others We did not bring to life, so they remained in the darknesses, not emerging from them, nor do their beautified deeds benefit them or save them from it. Likewise, We have placed in every town the major sinners (أكابر مجرميها) to plot therein against the religious call, the Prophet, and the believers; but it does not benefit them, for they are in darknesses and do not see; rather, they only plot against themselves and they do not perceive.

Accordingly, His saying: **كَذلِكَ زُيِّنَ لِلْكافِرِينَ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** is intended to explain that their deeds, which are made to seem fair to them, do not benefit them in extracting them from the darknesses they are in. And His saying: **وَكَذلِكَ جَعَلْنا فِي كُلِّ قَرْيَةٍ** etc., is intended to explain that their deeds and their plotting do not harm others; rather, their plotting only falls upon themselves, and they do not perceive because of the darkness that has overwhelmed them.

It has been said: The meaning of the comparison in the verse is that just as We have narrated to you, the deeds of the disbelievers were made to seem fair to them, and just as that, We placed in every town the major sinners, and We made the plotter among the sinners just as We made the possessor of light among the believers. So whatever We did with those, We did with these, except that those were guided by their good choice (حسن اختيارهم) and these went astray by their bad choice (سوء اختيارهم), because in each of them, the "making" (الجعل) is in the sense of "becoming" (الصيرورة), except that the first is by grace (اللطف) and the second is by enabling (التمكين) the plotting. (End of quote). This is not free from being far-fetched from the context.

The "making" (الجعل) in His saying: **جَعَلْنا فِي كُلِّ قَرْيَةٍ أَكابِرَ مُجْرِمِيها** is like the "making" in His saying: **وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً**. Thus, it is more appropriate that it be in the sense of creation (الخلق). The meaning is: We created in every town its major sinners so that they might plot therein. Their plotting being the end (غاية) of creation and the purpose (غرضاً) of the making is similar to the entry into the Fire being a divine purpose in His saying: **وَلَقَدْ ذَرَأْنا لِجَهَنَّمَ كَثِيراً مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ** (Certainly We have winnowed for hell many of the jinn and humans) [7:179]. The discussion on the meaning of that has already passed in several places in this book. He specifically mentioned "its major sinners" (أكابر مجرميها) because the objective is to explain the return of the plot upon the plotters, and plotting against Allah and His signs only emanates from them. As for the minor sinners, who are the commonality of the people, they are merely followers and tails.

As for His saying: **وَما يَمْكُرُونَ إِلَّا بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ وَما يَشْعُرُونَ** (and they do not plot except against themselves, but they do not perceive), this is because plotting (المكر) is an action that conceals evil and harm intended for the one plotted against, to spoil his desired purpose, lead his effort astray, and nullify the success of his work. But Allah, the Glorified, has no purpose in His religious call, nor any benefit in it, except what returns to the invited themselves. So if a human plots against Allah and His signs to spoil the purpose of the call and prevent the success of the effort therein, he has only plotted against himself from where he does not perceive; and he himself is the one harmed by that, not his Lord.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذا جاءَتْهُمْ آيَةٌ قالُوا لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ** (And when a sign comes to them, they say, ‘We will not believe...) until His saying: **رِسالَتَهُ** (...His apostleship) [6:124]. Their saying: **لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ حَتَّى نُؤْتى مِثْلَ ما أُوتِيَ رُسُلُ اللهِ** (We will not believe until we are given the like of what was given to Allah’s apostles) means they want to be given the apostleship (الرسالة) itself with its components of religious call, rather than just religious knowledge of principles and branches. Otherwise, the appropriate wording would have been to say: "the like of what was given to the prophets of Allah" or something similar, like their saying: **لَوْ لا يُكَلِّمُنَا اللهُ أَوْ تَأْتِينا آيَةٌ** (Why does not Allah speak to us, or a sign come to us?) [2:118] and their saying: **لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْنَا الْمَلائِكَةُ أَوْ نَرى رَبَّنا** (Why have the angels not been sent down to us, or why do we not see our Lord?) [25:21].

So their intent is: "We will never believe until we are given the apostleship just as the messengers were given it." In this, there is a degree of mockery (الاستهزاء), for they were not believers in the apostleship to begin with. Thus, in a way, it is similar to their saying: **لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ عَلى رَجُلٍ مِنَ الْقَرْيَتَيْنِ عَظِيمٍ** (Why was not this Quran sent down to some great man from the two cities?) [43:31], just as the response to it is similar to the response there, which is His saying, the Exalted: **أَهُمْ يَقْسِمُونَ رَحْمَتَ رَبِّكَ** (Is it they who dispense the mercy of your Lord?) [43:32], like His saying here: **اللهُ أَعْلَمُ حَيْثُ يَجْعَلُ رِسالَتَهُ** (Allah knows best where to place His apostleship).

From what has preceded, it becomes clear that the pronoun in His saying: **وَإِذا جاءَتْهُمْ آيَةٌ قالُوا** etc., refers back to "the major sinners" (أكابر مجرميها) in the previous verse. For if it referred to the general polytheists, their saying: **حَتَّى نُؤْتى مِثْلَ ما أُوتِيَ رُسُلُ اللهِ** would be nonsensical, as there is no meaning to an apostleship for all people where there is no one to whom they are sent. Furthermore, His saying: **اللهُ أَعْلَمُ حَيْثُ يَجْعَلُ رِسالَتَهُ** would not have been in its proper place; rather, the appropriate response would have been that it is nonsensical speech, as you have seen.

This is supported by the threat at the end of the verse: **سَيُصِيبُ الَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا صَغارٌ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَعَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِما كانُوا يَمْكُرُونَ** (Soon the guilty will be smitten by a humiliation from Allah and a severe punishment because of the plots they used to devise), where He described them with guilt (الإجرام) and justified the threat by their plotting, and He did not attribute plotting in the previous verse except to "the major sinners." Humiliation (الصغار) is ignominy and abasement.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَمَنْ يُرِدِ اللهُ أَنْ يَهْدِيَهُ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلامِ** (Whomever Allah desires to guide, He opens his breast to Islam) [6:125]. Opening (الشرح) is expansion (البسط). Al-Raghib mentioned in his *Mufradat* that its origin is the expansion of meat and the like. The opening of the breast (شرح الصدر)—which is considered in speech as the vessel for knowledge and gnosis—is its widening such that it can accommodate the true knowledge it encounters and does not reject the word of truth when it is presented to it. This is indicated by what is mentioned in the description of misguidance by contrast, which is His saying: **يَجْعَلْ صَدْرَهُ ضَيِّقاً حَرَجاً** (He makes his breast narrow and straitened) etc. So whomever Allah has opened his breast to Islam (الإسلام)—which is submission (التسليم) to Allah, the Glorified—He has expanded his breast and widened it to submit to whatever comes to him from Allah's side, whether a true belief or a righteous religious deed. No word of truth is presented to him but that he retains it, and no righteous deed but that he takes it up. This is only because for the eye of his insight (بصيرته), there is a light (نور) that falls upon the true belief and illuminates it, or the righteous deed and makes it shine. This is unlike the one whose heart's eye is blind, who cannot distinguish truth from falsehood or sincerity from lying. The Exalted said: **فَإِنَّها لا تَعْمَى الْأَبْصارُ وَلكِنْ تَعْمَى الْقُلُوبُ الَّتِي فِي الصُّدُورِ** (For indeed it is not the eyes that turn blind, but the hearts turn blind—those that are in the breasts) [22:46].

Allah, the Exalted, has explained the opening of the breast with this explanation in His saying: **أَفَمَنْ شَرَحَ اللهُ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلامِ فَهُوَ عَلى نُورٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ فَوَيْلٌ لِلْقاسِيَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ مِنْ ذِكْرِ اللهِ** (Is he whose breast Allah has opened to Islam so that he follows a light from his Lord [like him who is hard-hearted]? So woe to those whose hearts are hardened against the remembrance of Allah!) [39:22]. He described it and defined it by stating that its possessor is riding a light from Allah that shines before him in his path. Then He defined it by contrast as a softness in the heart (لينة في القلب) by which he accepts the remembrance of Allah and does not reject it due to hardness. Then He said: **اللهُ نَزَّلَ أَحْسَنَ الْحَدِيثِ كِتاباً مُتَشابِهاً مَثانِيَ تَقْشَعِرُّ مِنْهُ جُلُودُ الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ ثُمَّ تَلِينُ جُلُودُهُمْ وَقُلُوبُهُمْ إِلى ذِكْرِ اللهِ ذلِكَ هُدَى اللهِ يَهْدِي بِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَمَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللهُ فَما لَهُ مِنْ هادٍ** (Allah has sent down the best of discourses, a scripture [with parts] resembling each other, [and] paired, whereat quiver the skins of those who fear their Lord, then their skins and hearts soften to Allah’s remembrance. That is Allah’s guidance: He guides with it whomever He wishes; and whomever Allah leads astray, has no guide) [39:23]. He mentioned the softening of the heart to the remembrance of Allah and its obedience to the truth, and stated that this is the divine guidance (الهدى الإلهي) by which He guides whomever He wills. At that point, the two verses—the verse of *al-Zumar* and the verse we are currently in—return to a single meaning: that when Allah, the Glorified, guides one of His servants, He expands his breast so that it accommodates every true belief and righteous deed, accepting it with softness and not rejecting it with hardness. This is a type of spiritual light (النور المعنوي) that illuminates the true word and the righteous deed, and aids its possessor in holding fast to what it has illuminated. This is a marker (معرف) by which divine guidance is recognized.

From here, it becomes clear that the verse—meaning His saying: **فَمَنْ يُرِدِ اللهُ أَنْ يَهْدِيَهُ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلامِ**—is in the position of another explanation for His saying: **أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ**. The derivation (التفريع) in His saying **فَمَنْ يُرِدِ اللهُ** etc., is of the type of deriving one of two explanations from the other by claiming it is its result, as if the correspondence between the two explanations makes one of them a result following and a branch derived from the other—which is a subtle consideration (عناية لطيفة).

The meaning is: If the one whom Allah brought to life after he was dead is of this attribute—namely, that he follows a light from his Lord by which the necessary belief and action are illuminated for him, so he takes them up—then whomever Allah desires to guide, He widens his breast to submit to his Lord and not to disdain His worship. Thus, Islam is a light from Allah, and those who submit to their Lord follow a light from their Lord.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَنْ يُرِدْ أَنْ يُضِلَّهُ يَجْعَلْ صَدْرَهُ ضَيِّقاً** (and whomever He desires to lead astray, He makes his breast narrow...) to the end of the verse. Leading astray (الإضلال) is the opposite of guidance; therefore, its effect is the opposite of its effect, which is the narrowing (التضييق) that is opposite to the opening and expansion. Its effect is that it does not accommodate the truth and sincerity directed toward it, and it is straitened (يتحرج) from their entry into it. For this reason, He followed the breast being "narrow" (ضيقاً) with it being "straitened" (حرجاً).

Straitness (الحرج), according to what is in *al-Majma'*, is the narrowest of narrowness. Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: The origin of *al-haraj* and *al-hiraj* is the gathering of things, and from it is conceived the narrowness between them; thus, narrowness is called *haraj*, and sin is called *haraj*. (End of quote).

So His saying: **حَرَجاً كَأَنَّما يَصَّعَّدُ فِي السَّماءِ** (straitened as if he were climbing into the sky) is in the position of interpreting His saying **ضَيِّقاً** (narrow), and an indication that this is a type of narrowness that resembles, in a way, the narrowing and straitening observed in vessels and containers when one attempts to insert and place within them something greater than them.

And His saying: **كَذلِكَ يَجْعَلُ اللهُ الرِّجْسَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ** (Thus does Allah lay abomination on those who do not believe) [6:125] provides a universal rule (ضابط كلي) regarding the misguidance of those who do not believe: that they lose the state of submission to Allah and compliance with the truth. He used the term "lack of faith" (عدم الإيمان) generally, even though the context of the verses is the lack of faith in Allah, the Glorified—which is polytheism (الشرك)—but the explanation that preceded in the verse includes the lack of faith in Allah (polytheism) and the lack of faith in the signs of Allah—which is the rejection of some of what Allah has sent down of knowledge and laws. He indicated all of that by His saying: **يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلامِ** etc., and by His previous saying: **وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ** etc., and His saying: **يَجْعَلْ صَدْرَهُ ضَيِّقاً حَرَجاً** etc., and by His previous saying: **فِي الظُّلُماتِ لَيْسَ بِخارِجٍ مِنْها**.

In the verse, the misguidance that is synonymous with the lack of faith is called "abomination" (الرجس). Abomination (الرجس) is filth (القذر), except that there is considered in it a type of overcoming (استعلاء) indicated by His saying: **عَلَى الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ** (on those who do not believe), as if the abomination rises above them and surrounds them, intervening between them and others, so that natures are repelled by them just as they are repelled by food smeared with filth.

This verse has been used as evidence that guidance and misguidance are from Allah and that no one else has any part in them. This is an error, for the verse—as you have seen—is in the position of explaining the reality of the guidance and misguidance that are from Allah, and is a type of definition and delimitation for them, not in the position of explaining their restriction to Him and their negation from others, as is claimed. This is apparent.

Similar to this is what some have mentioned: that just as the verse indicates by its wording our view that guidance and misguidance are from Allah, it also indicates by its wording the definitive rational proof (الدليل العقلي) on this issue.

Its explanation: The servant is capable of both faith and disbelief equally; thus, the issuance of one of them from him instead of the other is impossible unless it is accompanied by a preponderant factor (مرجح) that necessitates the issuance of what is preponderated. This is the heart's motive (الداعي القلبي), which is nothing but knowledge, belief, or opinion that the action contains an additional benefit and a preponderant advantage without an additional harm or a preponderant corruption. We have already explained by proof that the occurrence of these motives in the heart is only from Allah, the Exalted, and that the combination of capacity and motive necessitates the action.

If this is established, we say: The issuance of faith from the servant is impossible unless Allah creates in his heart the belief in the preponderance of faith, and with it, there occurs from the heart an inclination toward it and from the soul a desire for it—and this is the opening of the breast. And disbelief is impossible except by His creating the opposite of that in the heart, whereupon there occurs an aversion to it and a disgust for it—and this is what is meant by making the heart narrow and straitened. Thus, the estimation of the verse becomes: Whomever Allah wants faith from, He strengthens his motives toward it; and whomever He wants disbelief from, He strengthens his deterrents from faith and strengthens his motives toward disbelief. Since it has been established by rational proof that the matter is so, it is established that the wording of the Quran contains these rational proofs. (End of summary).

In this, there is, firstly: that the attribution of a thing to Him, the Exalted, from the perspective of His creating the causes of its existence and its preliminaries, does not necessitate the negation of its attribution to other than Him, the Exalted. Otherwise, that would necessitate the nullification of the general law of causality (قانون العلية العام), and by its nullification, rational judgment would be nullified from the start. Thus, it is possible for guidance and misguidance to be attributed to other than Him, the Exalted, with a real attribution, while they are simultaneously attributed to Him, the Exalted, with a real attribution, without contradiction.

Secondly: that what the verse mentioned of His act, the Exalted, in the two cases of His guidance and His misguidance, is the expansion and narrowness of the heart, and these are not the desire of the soul and its aversion at all. Thus, the verse is entirely foreign to what he mentioned. The mere fact that the willing of an action by the servant entails his desire for it, and his dislike of it entails his aversion to it, does not necessitate that what is meant by the expansion and narrowness of the heart is the will and dislike regarding actions. In this, there is a fallacy of taking one of two concurrent things in place of the other. A strange statement of his is that the correspondence of the rational proof—which he established in his own view—with the verse necessitates the indication of the verse's wording upon it.

Thirdly: that you have seen that the verse is only in the position of defining what Allah does with His servant when He intends his guidance or his misguidance. As for whether every guidance or misguidance is from Allah, the Glorified, and not from anyone else, that is a matter foreign to the purpose of the verse. Thus, the verse has no indication that guidance and misguidance are from Allah, the Glorified, even if that is the truth.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَهذا صِراطُ رَبِّكَ مُسْتَقِيماً** (This is the straight path of your Lord) [6:126] to the end of the verse. The indication is to what was previously explained in the preceding verse regarding His act during guidance and misguidance. The meaning of the path (الصراط) and its straightness (استقامته) has already passed. Allah has explained in the verse that what He mentioned of opening the breast to Islam when He intends guidance, and making the breast narrow and straitened when intending misguidance, is His straight path and His ongoing sunnah (سنته الجارية) which does not vary and does not fail. For there is no believer except that his breast is opened to Islam by Allah, and the non-believer is the opposite of that.

So His saying: **وَهذا صِراطُ رَبِّكَ مُسْتَقِيماً** is a second explanation and a confirmation that the mentioned marker in the previous verse is a comprehensive and exclusive marker for guidance and misguidance. Then He, the Glorified, confirmed the explanation by His saying: **قَدْ فَصَّلْنَا الْآياتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَذَّكَّرُونَ** (We have certainly elaborated the signs for a people who take admonition). That is: the statement is a clear truth for one who takes admonition and returns to what Allah has deposited in his soul of primordial knowledge (المعارف الفطرية) and primary beliefs (العقائد الأولية), by the remembrance of which man is guided to the knowledge of every truth and its distinction from falsehood. Nevertheless, the explanation is for Allah, the Glorified, for He is the one who guides man to the conclusion after guiding him to the proof.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَهُمْ دارُ السَّلامِ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ وَهُوَ وَلِيُّهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** (For them is the abode of peace with their Lord, and He is their guardian because of what they used to do) [6:127]. What is meant by "peace" (السلام) is its linguistic meaning—as the apparent context suggests—which is being free from outward and inward afflictions. The "abode of peace" (دار السلام) is the place where no affliction threatens whoever enters it, such as death, disability, illness, poverty, or any other non-existence, loss, grief, or sorrow. This is the promised Paradise (الجنة), especially considering its restriction by His saying: **عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ** (with their Lord).

Yes, the friends of Allah (أولياء الله) find in this world what Allah promised them of dwelling them in the abode of peace, because they see that the kingdom belongs to Allah, so they do not own anything such that they would fear its loss or grieve for its absence. The Exalted said: **أَلا إِنَّ أَوْلِياءَ اللهِ لا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ** (Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve) [10:62]. They have no occupation except with their Lord; they have secluded themselves with Him in their lives. Thus, for them is the abode of peace with their Lord—while they are residing in this world—and He is their guardian (وليُّهم) because of what they used to do, which is their journeying in life by the light of divine guidance that He placed in their hearts and by which He illuminated their eyes and their insights.

It has been said that what is meant by "Peace" (السلام) is Allah, and His abode is Paradise, but the context rejects this. The plural pronouns in the verse refer back to the "people" in His saying: **لِقَوْمٍ يَذَّكَّرُونَ**—as has been said—because it is the nearest referent for them to return to. However, reflection upon the verses supports their return to those guided by the mentioned guidance, since the discourse is about them and the verses are intended to explain the goodness of Allah's act toward them. Thus, the mentioned good promise must return to them. As for the people who take admonition, they were only mentioned and entered into the purpose of the discourse secondarily.

## (A Discourse on the Meaning of Divine Guidance)

Guidance (الهداية) in the sense we know, however it is taken, is among the titles by which actions are titled and described. You say: "I guided so-and-so to such a matter" if you mentioned to him the manner of reaching it or showed him the path that ends at it—and this is guidance in the sense of "showing the way" (إراءة الطريق). Or you took him by the hand and accompanied him on the path until you brought him to the desired goal—and this is guidance in the sense of "conveying to the objective" (الإيصال إلى المطلوب).

What occurs externally in all these cases are types of actions that you perform, such as mentioning the path, showing it, or walking with the guided one. As for "guidance," it is a title for the action that revolves around the intention, just as what the guided one performs of action in its wake is titled with the title of "being guided" (الاهتداء). Thus, what is attributed to Him, the Exalted, of guidance, and for which He is called "the Guide" (الهادي)—which is one of the Beautiful Names—is among the attributes of action (صفات الفعل) abstracted from the act of Him, the Exalted, like mercy (الرحمة), provision (الرزق), and the like.

His guidance, the Exalted, is of two types: One is the ontological guidance (الهداية التكوينية), which relates to ontological matters, such as His guiding every species of created things to its perfection for which it was created and to its actions that were written for it, and His guiding every individual of creation to the matter decreed for it and the term set for its existence. The Exalted said: **الَّذِي أَعْطى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى** (He who gave everything its creation and then guided it) [20:50] and He said: **الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّى ، وَالَّذِي قَدَّرَ فَهَدى** (Who created and proportioned, who determined and guided) [87:2-3].

The second type: Legislative guidance (الهداية التشريعية), which relates to legislative matters such as true beliefs and righteous deeds that Allah, the Glorified, has established for command, prohibition, encouragement, and deterrent, and for which He promised reward upon taking them up and threatened punishment upon leaving them.

Among this guidance is that which is "showing the way," as in His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّا هَدَيْناهُ السَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شاكِراً وَإِمَّا كَفُوراً** (Indeed We have guided him to the way, be he grateful or ungrateful) [76:3].

And among it is that which is in the sense of "conveying to the objective," as in His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْ شِئْنا لَرَفَعْناهُ بِها وَلكِنَّهُ أَخْلَدَ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ وَاتَّبَعَ هَواهُ** (Had We wished, We would have surely raised him by their means, but he clung to the earth and followed his low desires) [7:176]. Allah, the Glorified, has defined this guidance with a definition in His saying: **فَمَنْ يُرِدِ اللهُ أَنْ يَهْدِيَهُ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلامِ** (Whomever Allah desires to guide, He opens his breast to Islam) [6:125]. It is a specific expansion in the heart by which it retains the true word and the righteous deed without being narrowed by them, and a specific preparedness by which it does not refuse submission to Allah's command and is not straitened by His judgment.

To this meaning, He, the Exalted, refers by His saying: **أَفَمَنْ شَرَحَ اللهُ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلامِ فَهو عَلى نُورٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ** until He said: **ذلِكَ هُدَى اللهِ يَهْدِي بِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ** (Is he whose breast Allah has opened to Islam so that he follows a light from his Lord [like him who is hard-hearted]? ... That is Allah’s guidance: He guides with it whomever He wishes) [39:22-23]. He described it in the verse as "light" (النور) because through it, there is revealed to the heart what it must retain of submission to the truth of speech and the sincerity of action, as opposed to what it must not retain or accept, which is the falsehood of speech and the corruption of action.

Allah, the Glorified, has drawn another sketch for this guidance, which is in His saying after mentioning His guidance of His noble prophets and what He specifically granted them of great blessings: **وَاجْتَبَيْناهُمْ وَهَدَيْناهُمْ إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ ذلِكَ هُدَى اللهِ يَهْدِي بِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ** (And We chose them and guided them to a straight path. That is Allah’s guidance: He guides with it whomever He wishes of His servants) [6:87-88]. We have clarified in the commentary of the verse that it indicates that among the specificities of divine guidance is that it brings those guided by it to a straight path (صراطاً مستقيماً) and an even way in which there is no failure and no variation.

Thus, no parts of His path—which is His religion (دينه) with its knowledge and laws—contradict other parts, since all of them represent pure monotheism (التوحيد الخالص), which is nothing but a single, fixed reality; and since all of them are built upon the divine primordial nature (الفطرة الإلهية), which does not err in its judgment and does not change in itself or in its requirements.

Nor do some of those riding upon it and journeying therein differ from others. That which one prophet of Allah calls to is the same as what all of them call to; and that which their Seal and the last of them invites to is the same as what their Adam and the first of them invited to, without any difference except in terms of brevity and detail.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Zayd, who said: I heard Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) saying regarding the saying of Allah, Blessed and Exalted: **أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ ـ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ** — "He said: 'Dead (ميت), not knowing anything. **نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ** (a light by which he walks among the people): an Imam whom he follows. **كَمَنْ مَثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُماتِ لَيْسَ بِخارِجٍ مِنْها** (like one who is in a manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge): He said: 'The one who does not know the Imam.'"

**I say:** This is of the type of application and instantiation (الجري والانطباق). The context of the verse insists that life is faith and light is the divine guidance to the true word and the righteous deed.

Al-Suyuti narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Zayd ibn Aslam: "That the verse was revealed concerning 'Ammar ibn Yasir." It was also narrated from Ibn 'Abbas and Zayd ibn Aslam: "That it was revealed concerning 'Umar ibn al-Khattab and Abu Jahl ibn Hisham." The context rejects the verse being specific.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn Abi al-Dunya, Ibn Jarir, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Hakim, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Shu'ab* extracted from various paths from Ibn Mas'ud, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said when this verse was revealed: **فَمَنْ يُرِدِ اللهُ أَنْ يَهْدِيَهُ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلامِ** — He said: 'When Allah enters the light into the heart, it is opened and expanded.' They said: 'Is there a sign (آية) for that by which it is known?' He said: 'Turning (الإنابة) toward the abode of eternity, withdrawing (التجافي) from the abode of delusion, and preparing for death before the descent of death.'"

**I say:** It was also narrated by several commentators from a group of the Successors (التابعين) such as Abu Ja'far al-Mada'ini, al-Fadl, al-Hasan, and 'Abdullah ibn al-Sawwar from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

In *al-'Uyun*, with its chain of narration from Hamdan ibn Sulayman al-Naysaburi, who said: I asked Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: **فَمَنْ يُرِدِ اللهُ أَنْ يَهْدِيَهُ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلامِ**. He said: "Whomever Allah desires to guide by his faith in this world—and to His Paradise and the abode of His honor in the Hereafter—He opens his breast for submission (التسليم) to Allah, trust in Him, and tranquility in what He has promised of His reward until he is at peace with it. And whomever He desires to lead astray from His Paradise and the abode of His honor in the Hereafter—because of his disbelief in Him and his disobedience to Him in this world—He makes his breast narrow and straitened until he doubts his disbelief (1) and is agitated in his belief until he becomes as if he were climbing into the sky—thus does Allah lay abomination on those who do not believe."

**I say:** In the hadith, there are fine points indicating what we explained in the preceding commentary.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Sulayman ibn Khalid from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, when Allah, Mighty and Majestic, intends good for a servant, He marks (نكت) in his heart a mark of light, opens the ears of his heart, and entrusts an angel to him to keep him firm. And when He intends evil for a servant, He marks in his heart a black mark, closes the ears of his heart, and entrusts a devil to him to lead him astray." Then he recited this verse: **فَمَنْ يُرِدِ اللهُ أَنْ يَهْدِيَهُ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلامِ ـ وَمَنْ يُرِدْ أَنْ يُضِلَّهُ يَجْعَلْ صَدْرَهُ ضَيِّقاً حَرَجاً ـ كَأَنَّما يَصَّعَّدُ فِي السَّماءِ**.

**I say:** Al-'Ayyashi narrated it in his *Tafsir* as a disconnected report (مرسلاً), and al-Saduq in *al-Tawhid* with a connected chain from him (peace be upon him).

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from al-Halabi from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, the heart wavers (يتلجلج) within the chest seeking the truth. When it comes to it, it finds peace and settles." Then he recited: **فَمَنْ يُرِدِ اللهُ أَنْ يَهْدِيَهُ** until His saying: **فِي السَّماءِ**.

**I say:** Al-'Ayyashi narrated it in his *Tafsir* from Abu Jamilah from 'Abdullah ibn Ja'far from his brother Musa (peace be upon him).

In the *Tafsir* of al-'Ayyashi, from Abu Basir from Khaythamah, who said: I heard Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) saying: "Indeed, the heart turns from its place to its throat as long as it has not hit upon the truth. When it hits upon the truth, it settles." Then he joined his fingers and read this verse: **فَمَنْ يُرِدِ اللهُ أَنْ يَهْدِيَهُ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلامِ ـ وَمَنْ يُرِدْ أَنْ يُضِلَّهُ يَجْعَلْ صَدْرَهُ ضَيِّقاً حَرَجاً**.

He said: And Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) said to Musa ibn Ashyam: "Do you know what *al-haraj* is?" He said: I said: "No." Then he gestured (1) with his hand and joined his fingers like a solid thing (المصمت) into which nothing enters and from which nothing exits.

**I say:** Something close to it was narrated in *Tafsir al-Burhan* from al-Saduq, and al-Barqi narrated the beginning of the hadith in *al-Mahasin* from Khaythamah from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him). What he interpreted *al-haraj* with matches what was previously quoted from al-Raghib.

In *al-Ikhtisas*, with its chain of narration from Adam ibn al-Hurr, who said: Musa ibn Ashyam asked Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) while I was present about a verse in the Book of Allah, and he informed him of it. He did not leave until a man entered and asked him about that very same verse, and he informed him of the opposite of what he had informed Musa ibn Ashyam.

Then Ibn Ashyam said: "I was overcome by such feelings as Allah willed—until it was as if my heart were being sliced with knives. I said: 'We left Abu Qatadah, who does not err in a single letter—the *waw* and its like—and I have come to one who makes all this error!'" While I was in that state, another man entered and asked him about that very same verse, and he was informed of the opposite of what he had informed me and the opposite of what he had informed the one who asked after me. Then it cleared from me, and I knew that it was intentional, so I thought something to myself.

Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) turned to me and said: "O Ibn Ashyam, do not do such and such"—and he revealed my thought about the matter I had thought to myself. Then he said: "O Ibn Ashyam, indeed Allah delegated (فوض) to Solomon ibn David, saying: **هذا عَطاؤُنا فَامْنُنْ أَوْ أَمْسِكْ بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ** (This is Our bestowal: give or withhold without any reckoning) [38:39]. And He delegated to His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and He has delegated to us, O Ibn Ashyam. **فَمَنْ يُرِدِ اللهُ أَنْ يَهْدِيَهُ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلامِ ـ وَمَنْ يُرِدْ أَنْ يُضِلَّهُ يَجْعَلْ صَدْرَهُ ضَيِّقاً حَرَجاً**. Do you know what *al-haraj* is?" I said: "No." He gestured with his hand and joined his fingers: "It is the solid thing (المصمت) from which nothing exits and into which nothing enters."

**I say:** The issue of delegation (التفويض) to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the Imams from his progeny, although several hadiths have been narrated in its explanation, what this hadith indicates is their being informed of knowledge from the Book of Allah that is not restricted to one or two aspects, and their being empowered over it with permission to spread what they wish of it. This is benefited from the application of what he (peace be upon him) mentioned regarding the matter of Solomon ibn David of the delegation benefited from the noble verse. It is not unlikely that the recitation of the verse was an indication of that, although it is apparent that it was intended to explain the state of hearts in connection with what Musa ibn Ashyam was afflicted with of heart agitation and anxiety.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: Regarding the verse, he said: "He said: 'Like a tree surrounded by many trees—it is unable to cast its branches right or left—so it passes into the sky and its straitness (حرجه) continues.'"

**I say:** This also matches what al-Raghib interpreted as the meaning of *al-haraj*.

In the *Tafsir* of al-'Ayyashi, from Abu Basir from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: **كَذلِكَ يَجْعَلُ اللهُ الرِّجْسَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ**. He said: "It is doubt (الشك)."

**I say:** This is of the type of application (التطبيق) and the explanation of some instances (المصاديق).

__________________
(1) "his faith" (إيمانه) is apparent.
(1) It is as if the statement implies the meaning of gesturing, meaning: He gestured with his hand and joined his fingers, saying: "Like the solid thing..." etc.
